title: Automate M365 to Google Workspace Migrations with ADK multi-agents
summary: Google's Agent Development Kit (ADK) is a framework for building reliable multi-agent systems with support for sequential orchestration, self-correcting loops, context caching, and single-command deployment to Google's Agent Engine. The tutorial demonstrates automating enterprise migrations from Microsoft 365 to Google Workspace using ADK's SequentialAgent and LoopAgent components.
writeup: A practical walkthrough of Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) through a concrete enterprise use case: mapping Microsoft 365 licenses and add-ons to Google Workspace equivalents and producing a structured 10-chapter report. The video argues that reliable agents require state management, routing, and guardrails, not just raw API calls, and demonstrates how ADK's sequential and loop agent primitives enforce that discipline. The through-line is modularity: decomposing a hard single-prompt task into small focused sub-agents, adding a self-correcting review loop, and caching a large knowledge base to control cost and latency.
key_points: ADK's sequential agent breaks the license-mapping pipeline into three focused sub-agents (input parser, researchers, reporter), each with its own system prompt, which reduces hallucinations and makes debugging easier than a single large prompt.

A self-correcting loop pairs the License Mapper with a report reviewer: the reviewer checks for missing features and broken formatting and bounces the output back to the mapper before the run finishes, replacing one-shot validation with an iterative correction cycle.

Context caching in ADK loads a large local CSV knowledge base once and reuses it across runs, avoiding context-window bloat and the API cost spike that comes from injecting the full file on every call.
The agent combines Google Search for real-time grounding with a locally stored enterprise feature matrix, giving it both current web data and a controlled authoritative knowledge base.
HTTP 429 resource exhaustion errors are prevented by adding two HTTP headers that activate priority inference, giving the agent guaranteed and prioritized slots at the model endpoint under load.
Deployment to Google Agent Engine requires a single command because ADK abstracts away custom Docker files and hosting infrastructure, making the path from local IDE to production direct.
